title: Flugelhorn

infobox.value: ([/ˈfluːɡəlhɔːrn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), also spelled **fluegelhorn**, **flugel horn**, or **flügelhorn**, is a [brass instrument](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument") that resembles the [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet") and [cornet](/wiki/Cornet "Cornet"), but has a wider, more [conical bore](/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments)#Conical_bore "Bore (wind instruments)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Like trumpets and cornets, most flugelhorns are pitched in B♭, though some are in C.[[2]](#cite_note-Britannica-2) It is a type of valved [bugle](/wiki/Bugle "Bugle"), developed in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") in the early 19th century from a traditional English valveless bugle. The first version of a valved bugle was sold by [Heinrich Stölzel](/wiki/Heinrich_St%C3%B6lzel "Heinrich Stölzel") in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") in 1828.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The valved bugle provided [Adolphe Sax](/wiki/Adolphe_Sax "Adolphe Sax") (creator of the [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone")) with the inspiration for his B♭ soprano (contralto) [saxhorns](/wiki/Saxhorn "Saxhorn"), on which the modern-day flugelhorn is modelled.[[4]](#cite_note-Grove-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

outgoing_links.target_title: The New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians

The **flugelhorn** ([/ˈfluːɡəlhɔːrn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), also spelled **fluegelhorn**, **flugel horn**, or **flügelhorn**, is a [brass instrument](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument") that resembles the [trumpet](/wiki/Trumpet "Trumpet") and [cornet](/wiki/Cornet "Cornet"), but has a wider, more [conical bore](/wiki/Bore_(wind_instruments)#Conical_bore "Bore (wind instruments)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Like trumpets and cornets, most flugelhorns are pitched in B♭, though some are in C.[[2]](#cite_note-Britannica-2) It is a type of valved [bugle](/wiki/Bugle "Bugle"), developed in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") in the early 19th century from a traditional English valveless bugle. The first version of a valved bugle was sold by [Heinrich Stölzel](/wiki/Heinrich_St%C3%B6lzel "Heinrich Stölzel") in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") in 1828.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The valved bugle provided [Adolphe Sax](/wiki/Adolphe_Sax "Adolphe Sax") (creator of the [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone")) with the inspiration for his B♭ soprano (contralto) [saxhorns](/wiki/Saxhorn "Saxhorn"), on which the modern-day flugelhorn is modelled.[[4]](#cite_note-Grove-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flugelhorn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The German word *Flügel* means *wing* or *flank* in English. In early 18th century Germany, a ducal hunt leader known as a *Flügelmeister* blew the *Flügelhorn*, a large semicircular [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") or [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") valveless horn, to direct the wings of the hunt. Military use dates from the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), where this instrument was employed as a predecessor of the bugle.[[4]](#cite_note-Grove-4)

## Structure and variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flugelhorn&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure and variants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Yamaha_Flugelhorn_YFH-436G.jpg/250px-Yamaha_Flugelhorn_YFH-436G.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yamaha_Flugelhorn_YFH-436G.jpg)

A rotary valve flugelhorn

The flugelhorn is generally pitched in B♭, like most trumpets and [cornets](/wiki/Cornet "Cornet"). It usually has three [piston valves](/wiki/Brass_instrument_valve "Brass instrument valve") and employs the same fingering system as other brass instruments, although four-valve versions and rotary-valve versions also exist. It can therefore be played by trumpet and cornet players, although it has different playing characteristics. The flugelhorn's [mouthpiece](/wiki/Mouthpiece_(brass) "Mouthpiece (brass)") is more deeply conical than either trumpet or cornet mouthpieces, but not as conical as a [French horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn") mouthpiece.

Some modern flugelhorns feature a fourth valve that lowers the pitch by a [perfect fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") (similar to the fourth valve on some [euphoniums](/wiki/Euphonium "Euphonium"), [tubas](/wiki/Tuba "Tuba"), and [piccolo trumpets](/wiki/Piccolo_trumpet "Piccolo trumpet"), or the [trigger](/wiki/Trombone#Valve_attachments "Trombone") on [trombones](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone")). This adds a useful low range that, coupled with the flugelhorn's dark sound, extends the instrument's abilities. Players can also use the fourth valve in place of the first and third valve combination (which is somewhat sharp).

A compact version of the [rotary valve](/wiki/Rotary_valve "Rotary valve") flugelhorn is the oval shaped [kuhlohorn](/wiki/Kuhlohorn "Kuhlohorn") in B♭. It was developed for the German Protestant trombone choirs.

A pair of bass flugelhorns in C, called [fiscorns](/wiki/Fiscorn "Fiscorn"), are played in the [Catalan](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") [cobla](/wiki/Cobla "Cobla") bands that provide music for [sardana](/wiki/Sardana "Sardana") dancers.

## Timbre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flugelhorn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Timbre")]

The tone is fatter and usually regarded as more mellow and dark than the trumpet or cornet. The sound of the flugelhorn has been described as halfway between a trumpet and a [French horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn"), whereas the cornet's sound is halfway between a trumpet and a flugelhorn.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The flugelhorn is as agile as the cornet but more difficult to control in the high register (from approximately written G5), where in general it locks onto notes less easily.

## Use and performances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flugelhorn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Use and performances")]

Flugelhorn excerpt



B♭ trumpet playing the same excerpt as above

The flugelhorn is a standard member of the [British-style brass band](/wiki/British_brass_band "British brass band"), and it is also used frequently in [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"). It also appears occasionally in [orchestral](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") and [concert band](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band") music. Famous orchestral works with flugelhorn include [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky")'s *[Threni](/wiki/Threni_(Stravinsky) "Threni (Stravinsky)")*,[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams")'s [Ninth Symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._9_(Vaughan_Williams) "Symphony No. 9 (Vaughan Williams)"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) and [Michael Tippett](/wiki/Michael_Tippett "Michael Tippett")'s third symphony.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The flugelhorn is sometimes substituted for the [post horn](/wiki/Post_horn "Post horn") in [Mahler's](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler") [Third Symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._3_(Mahler) "Symphony No. 3 (Mahler)"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) and for the soprano Roman [buccine](/wiki/Buccina "Buccina") in [Ottorino Respighi](/wiki/Ottorino_Respighi "Ottorino Respighi")'s *[Pines of Rome](/wiki/Pines_of_Rome "Pines of Rome")*. In [HK Gruber](/wiki/Heinz_Karl_Gruber "Heinz Karl Gruber")'s trumpet concerto *Busking* (2007) the soloist is directed to play a flugelhorn in the slow middle movement.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The flugelhorn figured prominently in many of [Burt Bacharach](/wiki/Burt_Bacharach "Burt Bacharach")'s 1960s [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") song arrangements. It is featured in a solo role in [Bert Kaempfert](/wiki/Bert_Kaempfert "Bert Kaempfert")'s 1962 recording of "That Happy Feeling". Flugelhorns have occasionally been used as the alto or low soprano voice in a [drum and bugle corps](/wiki/Drum_and_bugle_corps_(modern) "Drum and bugle corps (modern)").

Another use of the flugelhorn is found in the Dutch and Belgian "*Fanfareorkesten*" or [fanfare orchestras](/wiki/Fanfare_orchestra "Fanfare orchestra"). In these orchestras the flugelhorns, often between 10 and 20 in number, have a significant role, forming the base of the orchestra. They are pitched in B♭, with sporadically an E♭ soloist. Due to poor intonation, these E♭ flugelhorns are mostly replaced by the E♭ trumpet or cornet.

The 1996 film *[Brassed Off](/wiki/Brassed_Off "Brassed Off")* features a flugelhorn performance of [Rodrigo's](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Rodrigo "Joaquín Rodrigo") [Concierto de Aranjuez](/wiki/Concierto_de_Aranjuez "Concierto de Aranjuez"), Adagio, as a key moment.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The solo is played by Paul Hughes.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Notable players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flugelhorn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notable players")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Flugelhorn "Special:EditPage/Flugelhorn") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[Joe Bishop](/wiki/Joe_Bishop "Joe Bishop"), as a member of the [Woody Herman](/wiki/Woody_Herman "Woody Herman") band in 1936, was one of the earliest jazz musicians to use the flugelhorn. [Shorty Rogers](/wiki/Shorty_Rogers "Shorty Rogers") and [Kenny Baker](/wiki/Kenny_Baker_(trumpeter) "Kenny Baker (trumpeter)") began playing it in the early fifties, and [Clark Terry](/wiki/Clark_Terry "Clark Terry") used it in [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington")'s orchestra in the mid-1950s. [Chet Baker](/wiki/Chet_Baker "Chet Baker") recorded several albums on the instrument in the 1950s and 1960s. [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis") further popularized the instrument in jazz on the albums *[Miles Ahead](/wiki/Miles_Ahead_(album) "Miles Ahead (album)")* and *[Sketches of Spain](/wiki/Sketches_of_Spain "Sketches of Spain")*, (both arranged by [Gil Evans](/wiki/Gil_Evans "Gil Evans")) though he did not use it much on later projects. Other prominent flugelhorn players include [Hugh Masekela](/wiki/Hugh_Masekela "Hugh Masekela"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Donald Byrd](/wiki/Donald_Byrd "Donald Byrd"), Freddy Buzon, [Freddie Hubbard](/wiki/Freddie_Hubbard "Freddie Hubbard"), [Tom Browne](/wiki/Tom_Browne_(trumpeter) "Tom Browne (trumpeter)"), [Lee Morgan](/wiki/Lee_Morgan "Lee Morgan"), [Bill Dixon](/wiki/Bill_Dixon "Bill Dixon"), [Wilbur Harden](/wiki/Wilbur_Harden "Wilbur Harden"), [Art Farmer](/wiki/Art_Farmer "Art Farmer"), [Roy Hargrove](/wiki/Roy_Hargrove "Roy Hargrove"), [Randy Brecker](/wiki/Randy_Brecker "Randy Brecker"), [Hugh Masekela](/wiki/Hugh_Masekela "Hugh Masekela"), [Feya Faku](/wiki/Feya_Faku "Feya Faku"), [Tony Guerrero](/wiki/Tony_Guerrero "Tony Guerrero"), Gary Lord, [Jimmy Owens](/wiki/Jimmy_Owens_(musician) "Jimmy Owens (musician)"), [Maynard Ferguson](/wiki/Maynard_Ferguson "Maynard Ferguson"), [Terumasa Hino](/wiki/Terumasa_Hino "Terumasa Hino"), [Woody Shaw](/wiki/Woody_Shaw "Woody Shaw"), [Bobby Shew](/wiki/Bobby_Shew "Bobby Shew"), [Guido Basso](/wiki/Guido_Basso "Guido Basso"), [Kenny Wheeler](/wiki/Kenny_Wheeler "Kenny Wheeler"), [Tom Harrell](/wiki/Tom_Harrell "Tom Harrell"), [Bill Coleman](/wiki/Bill_Coleman_(trumpeter) "Bill Coleman (trumpeter)"), [Thad Jones](/wiki/Thad_Jones "Thad Jones"), [Arturo Sandoval](/wiki/Arturo_Sandoval "Arturo Sandoval"), [Lee Loughnane](/wiki/Lee_Loughnane "Lee Loughnane") of the rock band [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(band) "Chicago (band)"), [Roddy Lorimer](/wiki/Roddy_Lorimer "Roddy Lorimer") of the [Kick Horns](/wiki/Kick_Horns "Kick Horns"), [Mike Metheny](/wiki/Mike_Metheny "Mike Metheny"), [Harry Beckett](/wiki/Harry_Beckett "Harry Beckett"), [Till Brönner](/wiki/Till_Br%C3%B6nner "Till Brönner") and [Ack van Rooyen](/wiki/Ack_van_Rooyen "Ack van Rooyen"). Most jazz flugelhorn players use the instrument as an auxiliary to the trumpet, but in the 1970s [Chuck Mangione](/wiki/Chuck_Mangione "Chuck Mangione") gave up playing the trumpet and concentrated on the flugelhorn alone, notably on his [jazz-pop](/wiki/Jazz_fusion#Jazz_pop "Jazz fusion") hit song "[Feels So Good](/wiki/Feels_So_Good_(composition) "Feels So Good (composition)")". Mangione, in an interview on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") during the [1980 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics"), for which he wrote the theme "[Give It All You Got](/wiki/Give_It_All_You_Got "Give It All You Got")", referred to the flugelhorn as "the right baseball glove".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Pop flugelhorn players include Probyn Gregory ([Brian Wilson](/wiki/Brian_Wilson "Brian Wilson") Band), Ronnie Wilson of [The Gap Band](/wiki/The_Gap_Band "The Gap Band"), [Rick Braun](/wiki/Rick_Braun "Rick Braun"), [Mic Gillette](/wiki/Mic_Gillette "Mic Gillette"), [Jeff Oster](/wiki/Jeff_Oster "Jeff Oster"), Zach Condon of the band [Beirut](/wiki/Beirut_(band) "Beirut (band)"), [Scott Spillane](/wiki/Scott_Spillane "Scott Spillane") of the band [Neutral Milk Hotel](/wiki/Neutral_Milk_Hotel "Neutral Milk Hotel"), [Terry Kirkman](/wiki/Terry_Kirkman "Terry Kirkman") of the band [The Association](/wiki/The_Association "The Association"), Annie Chappell and [Rashawn Ross](/wiki/Rashawn_Ross "Rashawn Ross") of the [Dave Matthews Band](/wiki/Dave_Matthews_Band "Dave Matthews Band"). [Marvin Stamm](/wiki/Marvin_Stamm "Marvin Stamm") played the flugelhorn solo on "[Uncle Albert/Admiral Halsey](/wiki/Uncle_Albert/Admiral_Halsey "Uncle Albert/Admiral Halsey")" by [Paul](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") and [Linda McCartney](/wiki/Linda_McCartney "Linda McCartney").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Classical flugelhorn players include [Sergei Nakariakov](/wiki/Sergei_Nakariakov "Sergei Nakariakov") and Kirill Soldatov.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
